name: MATHEMATICS
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2024

Code: 65/3/3 (set-3)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Please check that this question paper contains 38 questions.
  3. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer book during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This Question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Question paper is divided into FIVE Sections - Section A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A - Question Number 1 to 18 are Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) and Question Number 19 and 20 are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B - Question Number 21 to 25 are Very Short Answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C - Question Number 26 to 31 are Short Answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D - Question Number 32 to 35 are Long Answer (LA) type questions, carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E - Question Number 36 to 38 are case study based questions, carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section - B, 3 questions in Section - C, 2 questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{D}{/tex} and {tex}{2}{/tex} questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{E}{/tex}.
  9. Use of calculator is not allowed.

Section - A

This section comprises multiple choice questions (MCQs) of 1 mark each.
1.

The value of {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll}8 & 2 & 7 \\ 12 & 3 & 5 \\ 16 & 4 & 3\end{array}\right|{/tex} is:

a. 0
b. 2
c. 7
d. -2
Correct Answer: (a) 0
Explanation:

0

 1
2.

If {tex}y=\sin ^{-1} x{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} is:

a. {tex}\sec y{/tex}
b. {tex}\sec {y} \tan {y}{/tex}
c. {tex}\sec ^{2} y \tan y{/tex}
d. {tex}\tan ^{2} y \sec y{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}\sec ^{2} y \tan y{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\sec ^{2} y \tan y{/tex}

 1
3.

If {tex}|\overrightarrow{{a}}|=2{/tex} and {tex}-3 \leq {k} \leq 2{/tex}, then {tex}|{k} \overrightarrow{{a}}| \in{/tex}:

a. {tex}[-6,4]{/tex}
b. {tex}[0,4]{/tex}
c. {tex}[4,6]{/tex}
d. {tex}[0,6]{/tex}
Correct Answer: (d) {tex}[0,6]{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}[0,6]{/tex}

 1
4.

If a line makes an angle of {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} with the positive directions of both x -axis and z -axis, then the angle which it makes with the positive direction of y-axis is:

a. 0
b. {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
d. {tex}\pi{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}

 1
5.

Of the following, which group of constraints represents the feasible region given below?

a. {tex}{x}+2 {y} \leq 76,2 {x}+{y} \geq 104, {x}, {y} \geq 0{/tex}
b. {tex}{x}+2 {y} \leq 76,2 {x}+{y} \leq 104, {x}, {y} \geq 0{/tex}
c. {tex}{x}+2 {y} \geq 76,2 {x}+{y} \leq 104, {x}, {y} \geq 0{/tex}
d. {tex}{x}+2 {y} \geq 76,2 {x}+{y} \geq 104, {x}, {y} \geq 0{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}{x}+2 {y} \geq 76,2 {x}+{y} \leq 104, {x}, {y} \geq 0{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}{x}+2 {y} \geq 76,2 {x}+{y} \leq 104, {x}, {y} \geq 0{/tex}

 1
6.

If {tex}{A}=\left[\begin{array}{lll}2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5\end{array}\right]{/tex}, then {tex}{A}^{-1}{/tex} is:

a. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}\frac{1}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1}{5}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
b. {tex}30\left[\begin{array}{lll}\frac{1}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1}{5}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{1}{30}\left[\begin{array}{lll}2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5\end{array}\right]{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{1}{30}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}\frac{1}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1}{5}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Correct Answer: (a) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}\frac{1}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1}{5}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}\frac{1}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{3} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \frac{1}{5}\end{array}\right]{/tex}

 1
7.

If {tex}{A}=\left[{a}_{{ij}}\right]{/tex} is an identity matrix, then which of the following is true?

a. {tex}{a}_{{ij}}= \begin{cases}0, & \text { if } {i}={j} \\ 1, & \text { if } {i} \neq {j}\end{cases}{/tex}
b. {tex}{a}_{{ij}}=1, \forall {i}, {j}{/tex}
c. {tex}{a}_{{ij}}=0, \forall {i}, {j}{/tex}
d. {tex}{a}_{{ij}}= \begin{cases}0, & \text { if } {i} \neq {j} \\ 1, & \text { if } {i}={j}\end{cases}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (d) {tex}{a}_{{ij}}= \begin{cases}0, & \text { if } {i} \neq {j} \\ 1, & \text { if } {i}={j}\end{cases}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}{a}_{{ij}}= \begin{cases}0, & \text { if } {i} \neq {j} \\ 1, & \text { if } {i}={j}\end{cases}{/tex}

 1
8.

Let Z denote the set of integers, then function {tex}{f}: {Z} \rightarrow {Z}{/tex} defined as {tex}{f}({x})={x}^{3}-1{/tex} is:

a. both one-one and onto
b. one-one but not onto
c. onto but not one-one
d. neither one-one nor onto
Correct Answer: (b) one-one but not onto
Explanation:

one-one but not onto

 1
9.

Let {tex}{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll}{a} & {b} \\ {c} & {d}\end{array}\right]{/tex} be a square matrix such that adj {tex}{A}={A}{/tex}. Then, {tex}({a}+{b}+{c}+{d}){/tex} is equal to:

a. 2a
b. 2 b
c. 2 c
d. 0
Correct Answer: (a) 2a
Explanation:

2a

 1
10.

A function {tex}{f}({x})=|1-{x}+|{x}||{/tex} is:

a. discontinuous at {tex}{x}=1{/tex} only
b. discontinuous at {tex}{x}=0{/tex} only
c. discontinuous at {tex}{x}=0,1{/tex}
d. continuous everywhere
Correct Answer: (d) continuous everywhere
Explanation:

continuous everywhere

 1
11.

The rate of change of surface area of a sphere with respect to its radius '{tex}r{/tex}', when {tex}r=4 {~cm}{/tex}, is:

a. {tex}64 \pi \ {cm}^{2} / {cm}{/tex}
b. {tex}48 \pi \ {cm}^{2} / {cm}{/tex}
c. {tex}32 \pi \ {cm}^{2} / {cm}{/tex}
d. {tex}16 \pi \ {cm}^{2} / {cm}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}32 \pi \ {cm}^{2} / {cm}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}32 \pi \ {cm}^{2} / {cm}{/tex}

 1
12.

{tex}\int_{-a}^{a} f(x) d x=0{/tex}, if:

a. {tex}{f}(-{x})={f}({x}){/tex}
b. {tex}f(-x)=-f(x){/tex}
c. {tex}f(a-x)=f(x){/tex}
d. {tex}f(a-x)=-f(x){/tex}
Correct Answer: (b) {tex}f(-x)=-f(x){/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}f(-x)=-f(x){/tex}

 1
13.

{tex}x \log x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=2 \log x{/tex} is an example of {tex}a{/tex}:

a. variable separable differential equation.
b. homogeneous differential equation.
c. first order linear differential equation.
d. differential equation whose degree is not defined.
Correct Answer: (c) first order linear differential equation.
Explanation:

first order linear differential equation.

14.

If {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}, then {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} are:

a. collinear vectors which are not parallel
b. parallel vectors
c. perpendicular vectors
d. unit vectors
Correct Answer: (c) perpendicular vectors
Explanation:

perpendicular vectors

15.

If {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} are the angles which a line makes with positive directions of {tex}{x}, {y}{/tex} and z axes respectively, then which of the following is not true?

a. {tex}\cos ^{2} \alpha+\cos ^{2} \beta+\cos ^{2} \gamma=1{/tex}
b. {tex}\sin ^{2} \alpha+\sin ^{2} \beta+\sin ^{2} \gamma=2{/tex}
c. {tex}\cos 2 \alpha+\cos 2 \beta+\cos 2 \gamma=-1{/tex}
d. {tex}\cos \alpha+\cos \beta+\cos \gamma=1{/tex}
Correct Answer: (d) {tex}\cos \alpha+\cos \beta+\cos \gamma=1{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\cos \alpha+\cos \beta+\cos \gamma=1{/tex}

 1
16.

The restrictions imposed on decision variables involved in an objective function of a linear programming problem are called:

a. feasible solutions
b. constraints
c. optimal solutions
d. infeasible solutions
Correct Answer: (b) constraints
Explanation:

constraints

17.

Let E and F be two events such that {tex}{P}({E})=0 .1, {P}({F})=0 .3, {P}({E} \cup {F})=0 .4{/tex}, then {tex}{P}({F} \mid {E}){/tex} is:

a. 0.6
b. 0.4
c. 0.5
d. 0
Correct Answer: (d) 0
Explanation:

0

 1
18.

If A and B are two skew symmetric matrices, then {tex}({AB}+{BA}){/tex} is:

a. a skew symmetric matrix
b. a symmetric matrix
c. a null matrix
d. an identity matrix
Correct Answer: (b) a symmetric matrix
Explanation:

a symmetric matrix

 1

Questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion and Reason based questions. Two statements are given, one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below.

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.
19.

Assertion (A): For any non-zero unit vector {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}, \overrightarrow{{a}} .(-\overrightarrow{{a}})=(-\overrightarrow{{a}}) .\overrightarrow{{a}}=-1{/tex}.
Reason {tex}(R){/tex}: Angle between {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}(-\overrightarrow{{a}}){/tex} is {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
Correct Answer: (c) A is true but R is false.
Explanation:

A is true but R is false.

 1
20.

Assertion (A): Every scalar matrix is a diagonal matrix.
Reason ({tex}R{/tex}): In a diagonal matrix, all the diagonal elements are 0.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
Correct Answer: (c) A is true but R is false.
Explanation:

A is true but R is false.

 1

Section - B

This section comprises very short answer (VSA) type questions of 2 marks each.
21.

{tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}, \overrightarrow{{b}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex} are three mutually perpendicular unit vectors. If {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}\overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and ({tex}2 \overrightarrow{{a}}+3 \overrightarrow{{~b}}+6 \overrightarrow{{c}}{/tex}), find the value of {tex}\cos \theta{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex} \text { Given }|\vec{a}|=|\vec{b}|={/tex}{tex}|\vec{c}|=1 \text { and } \vec{a} .\vec{b}={/tex}{tex}\vec{b} .\vec{c}=\vec{c} .\vec{a}=0 {/tex}
{tex} \text { Now, }|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|^2{/tex} {tex}=4|\vec{a}|^2+9|\vec{b}|^2+36|\vec{c}|^2=49 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow|2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|=7 {/tex}
{tex} \cos \theta=\frac{\vec{a} \cdot(2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c})}{|\vec{a}||2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|}=\frac{2|\vec{a}|^2}{|\vec{a}||2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}+6 \vec{c}|} {/tex}
{tex} \therefore \cos \theta=\frac{2}{7} {/tex}

 2
22.

Evaluate: {tex}\cot ^{2}\left\{\operatorname{cosec}^{-1} 3\right\}{/tex}{tex}+\sin ^{2}\left\{\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\right\}{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}{\left[\operatorname{cosec} e^{2}\left(\operatorname{cosec} e^{-1} 3\right)-1\right]}+{/tex}{tex}{\left[1-\cos ^{2}\left(\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}\right)\right]}{/tex}
{tex}=(9-1)+\left(1-\frac{1}{9}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{80}{9}{/tex}

 2
23.(i)

If {tex}{x}={e}^{{x} / {y}}{/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{\log {x}-1}{(\log {x})^{2}}{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}x=e^{\frac{x}{y}} \Rightarrow \log x=\frac{x}{y} \Rightarrow y=\frac{x}{\log x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{(\log x)(1)-x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}{(\log x)^{2}}=\frac{\log x-1}{(\log x)^{2}}{/tex}

 2

OR
(ii)

Check the differentiability of {tex}{f}({x})=\left\{\begin{array}{cr}{x}^{2}+1, & 0 \leq {x} \lt 1 \\ 3-{x}, & 1 \leq {x} \leq 2\end{array}\right.{/tex} at {tex}{x}=1{/tex}.

Solution:

LHD at {tex}x=1{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}{/tex}{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[(1-h)^{2}+1\right]-2}{-h}=2{/tex}
RHD at {tex}x=1{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}{/tex}{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[3-(1+h)]-2}{h}=-1{/tex}
as {tex}{LHD} \neq {RHD}{/tex}, so {tex}f(x){/tex} is not differentiable at {tex}x=1{/tex}

 2
24.(i)

Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 2} \sin 2 x \cos 3 x d x{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin 2 x \cos 3 x d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\sin 5 x-\sin x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{1}{5} \cos 5 x+\cos x\right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{2}{5}{/tex}

 2

OR
(ii)

Given {tex}\frac{d}{d x} F(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 x-x^{2}}}{/tex} and {tex}F(1)=0{/tex}, find {tex}F(x){/tex}.

Solution:

{tex} F(x)=\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 x-x^2}} d x {/tex}
{tex} =\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}} d x {/tex}
{tex} =\sin ^{-1}(x-1)+c {/tex}
{tex} \text { when } x=1, {/tex}{tex}y=0 \text { gives } c=0 {/tex}
{tex} \therefore F(x)=\sin ^{-1}(x-1) {/tex}

 2
25.

Find the position vector of point C which divides the line segment joining points {tex}A{/tex} and {tex}B{/tex} having position vectors {tex}\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}-\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k}{/tex} respectively in the ratio {tex}4: 1{/tex} externally. Further, find {tex}|\overrightarrow{{AB}}|:|\overrightarrow{{BC}}|{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex} \text { Position vector of } C=\vec{r}=\frac{4 \vec{b}-\vec{a}}{3} {/tex}
{tex} \text { i.e. } \vec{r}=\frac{1}{3}(-5 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+5 \hat{k}) {/tex}
{tex} \text { Now, } \overrightarrow{A B}=-2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+2 \hat{k} \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{A B}|=3 {/tex}
{tex} \overrightarrow{B C}=-\frac{1}{3}(2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}) \Rightarrow|\overrightarrow{B C}|=1 {/tex}
{tex} |\overrightarrow{A B}|:|\overrightarrow{B C}|=3: 1 {/tex}

 2

Section - C

Questions no. 26 to 31 are short answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
26.

Solve the following linear programming problem graphically:
Maximize {tex}{z}={x}+{y}{/tex}
subject to constraints
{tex} 2 x+5 y \leq 100 {/tex}
{tex} 8 x+5 y \leq 200 {/tex}
{tex} x \geq 0, y \geq 0 {/tex}.

Solution:

Corner Point Value of z = 4x+3y
O(0,0) 0
A(25,0) 25
{tex}B\left(\frac{50}{3}, \frac{40}{3}\right){/tex} 30
C(0,20) 20

{tex}z_{\max }=30{/tex} when {tex}x=\frac{50}{3}, y=\frac{40}{3}{/tex}

 3
27.

The chances of {tex}{P}, {Q}{/tex} and R getting selected as CEO of a company are in the ratio {tex}4: 1: 2{/tex} respectively. The probabilities for the company to increase its profits from the previous year under the new CEO, P, Q or R are {tex}0 \cdot 3,0 \cdot 8{/tex} and {tex}0 \cdot 5{/tex} respectively. If the company increased the profits from the previous year, find the probability that it is due to the appointment of {tex}R{/tex} as CEO.

Solution:

Let {tex}E_1: P{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex},
{tex}E_2: Q{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex},
{tex}E_3: R{/tex} is appointed as {tex}C E O{/tex}
{tex}A{/tex} : company increase profits from previous year here, {tex}{P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{4}{7}, {P}\left(E_3\right)=\frac{1}{7}, {P}\left(E_1\right)=\frac{2}{7}{/tex}
{tex}P\left(A \mid E_1\right) =0.3, {/tex} {tex}P\left(A \mid E_2\right)=0.8, {/tex}{tex}P\left(A \mid E_3\right)=0.5{/tex}
{tex}P\left(E_3 \mid A\right) {/tex}{tex}=\frac{P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{P\left(E_1\right) P\left(A \mid E_1\right)+P\left(E_2\right) P\left(A \mid E_2\right)+P\left(E_3\right) P\left(A \mid E_3\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{2}{7} \times 0.5}{\frac{4}{7} \times 0.3+\frac{1}{7} \times 0.8+\frac{2}{7} \times 0.5}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

 3
28.(i)

If {tex}x \cos (p+y)+\cos p \sin (p+y)=0{/tex}, prove that {tex}\cos p \frac{d y}{d x}=-\cos ^{2}(p+y){/tex}, where {tex}p{/tex} is a constant.

Solution:

{tex} x \cos (p+y)+\cos p \sin (p+y)=0 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{-\cos p \sin (p+y)}{\cos (p+y)} \Rightarrow x=-\cos p \cdot \tan (p+y) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=-\cos p \cdot \sec ^2(p+y) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\cos p \cdot \sec ^2(p+y)} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos p \frac{d y}{d x}=-\cos ^2(p+y) {/tex}

 3

OR
(ii)

Find the value of a and b so that function f defined as:
{tex}f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{x-2}{|x-2|}+a, & \text { if } x \lt 2 \\ a+b, & \text { if } x=2 \\ \frac{x-2}{|x-2|}+b, & \text { if } x \gt 2\end{cases}{/tex} is a continuous function.

Solution:

{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{x-2}{-(x-2)}+a & ; x<2 \\ a+b & ; x=2 \\ \frac{x-2}{(x-2)}+b & ; x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -1+a & ; x<2 \\ a+b & ; x=2 \\ 1+b & ; x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex} \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=-1+a, \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=1+b \text { and } f(2)=a+b {/tex}
as f is continous at x = 2
{tex} \therefore-1+a=1+b=a+b{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow a=1, b=-1 {/tex}

 3
29.(i)

Find the intervals in which the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} is strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

Solution:

Given function {tex}\log {x} / {x}{/tex}
{tex} f(x)=\frac{\log x}{x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x(1 / x)-\log x(1)}{x^2}=\frac{1-\log x}{x^2} {/tex}
{tex} f^{\prime}(x)>0 \Rightarrow \frac{1-\log x}{x^2}>0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\log x>0 \Rightarrow \log x<1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x<e \text { and } x>0 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore f(x){/tex} is increasing in ({tex}0, e{/tex})
{tex} f^{\prime}(x)<0 \Rightarrow \frac{1-\log x}{x^2}<0 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\log x<0 \Rightarrow \log x>1 \Rightarrow x>e{/tex}
{tex}\therefore f(x){/tex} is decreasing in ({tex}e, \infty{/tex}).

 3

OR
(ii)

Find the absolute maximum and absolute minimum values of the function f given by {tex}{f}({x})=\frac{{x}}{2}+\frac{2}{{x}}{/tex}, on the interval {tex}[1,2]{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}f(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x} ; x \in[1,2]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{I}(x)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{2}{x^{2}}{/tex}
for absolute maximum/minimum, put {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2}=4 \Rightarrow x=2{/tex}
Now, {tex}f(1)=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and {tex}f(2)=2{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} absolute maximum value {tex}=\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and absolute minimum value {tex}=2{/tex}

 3
30.

Find: {tex}\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{(x+1)(x-1)} d x{/tex}

Solution:

{tex} I=\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{(x+1)(x-1)} d x {/tex}
{tex} \sqrt{x}=t \text { gives } I=\int \frac{2 t^2}{\left(t^2+1\right)\left(t^2-1\right)} d t {/tex}
Let {tex}\frac{2 t^2}{\left(t^2+1\right)\left(t^2-1\right)}=\frac{2 z}{(z+1)(z-1)} ;{/tex} where {tex}t^2=z{/tex}
{tex} \text { we have } \frac{2 z}{(z+1)(z-1)}=\frac{1}{z+1}+\frac{1}{z-} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow I =\int \frac{1}{t^2+1} d t+\int \frac{1}{t^2-1} d t {/tex}
{tex} =\tan ^{-1} t+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{t-1}{t+1}\right|+c {/tex}
{tex} =\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}+\frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{\sqrt{x}-1}{\sqrt{x}+1}\right|+c {/tex}

 3
31.(i)

Find: {tex}\int \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x} e^{x} d x{/tex}

Solution:

We are given
{tex} \int \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x} e^x d x {/tex}
Step 1: Simplify the trigonometric part
Use identities:
{tex} 1+\cos 2 x=2 \cos ^2 x, \sin 2 x=2 \sin x \cos x {/tex}
{tex} \frac{2+\sin 2 x}{1+\cos 2 x}=\frac{2+2 \sin x \cos x}{2 \cos ^2 x}=\frac{1}{\cos ^2 x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}=\sec ^2 x+\tan x {/tex}
Step 2: Substitute
{tex} \int e^x\left(\sec ^2 x+\tan x\right) d x {/tex}
Observe that:
{tex} \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^x \tan x\right)=e^x \tan x+e^x \sec ^2 x {/tex}
Step 3: Write the result
{tex}\int e^x\left(\sec ^2 x+\tan x\right) d x=e^x \tan x+C{/tex}
{tex}=e^x \cdot \tan x+c{/tex}

 3

OR
(ii)

Evaluate: {tex}\int_{0}^{\pi / 4} \frac{1}{\sin x+\cos x} d x{/tex}

Solution:

{tex} I=\int\limits_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\sin x+\cos x} d x {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int\limits_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\cos \frac{\pi}{4} \sin x+\sin \frac{\pi}{4} \cos x} d x {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int\limits_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{1}{\sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \int\limits_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \operatorname{cosec}\left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right) d x {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left[\log \left(\left.\operatorname{cosec}\left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)-\cot \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \right\rvert\,\right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}}\right. {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \log (\sqrt{2}+1) \text { or }-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \log (\sqrt{2}-1) {/tex}

 3

Section - D

Questions no. 32 to 35 are long answer (LA) type questions carrying 5 marks each.
32.(i)

Find the equation of the line passing through the point of intersection of the lines {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y}{-3}=\frac{z-7}{2}{/tex} and perpendicular to these given lines.

Solution:

The first line is given by {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y-1}{2}=\frac{z-2}{3}=\lambda{/tex}.
The coordinates of any point on the first line are given by {tex}x=\lambda, y=2 \lambda+1{/tex}, and {tex}z=3 \lambda+2{/tex}.
The second line is given by {tex}\frac{x-1}{0}=\frac{y}{-3}=\frac{z-7}{2}=\mu{/tex}.
The coordinates of any point on the second line are given by {tex}x=1, y=-3 \mu{/tex}, and {tex}z=2 \mu+7{/tex}.
To find the point of intersection, the coordinates must be equal.
Equating the {tex}x{/tex}-coordinates gives {tex}\lambda=1{/tex}.
Equating the {tex}y{/tex}-coordinates gives {tex}2 \lambda+1=-3 \mu{/tex}.
Substituting {tex}\lambda=1{/tex} into the {tex}y{/tex}-coordinate equation gives {tex}2(1)+1=-3 \mu{/tex}, which simplifies to {tex}3=-3 \mu{/tex}.
Solving for {tex}\mu{/tex} gives {tex}\mu=-1{/tex}.
The {tex}z{/tex}-coordinates are checked for consistency.
For the first line, {tex}z=3 \lambda+2=3(1)+2=5{/tex}.
For the second line, {tex}z=2 \mu+7=2(-1)+7=5{/tex}.
The {tex}z{/tex}-coordinates are equal, so the point of intersection is {tex}(1,3,5){/tex}.

Direction Ratios of the Required Line
The direction ratios of the first line are {tex}(1,2,3){/tex}.
The direction ratios of the second line are {tex}(0,-3,2){/tex}.
The required line is perpendicular to both given lines.
The direction vector of the required line is the cross product of the direction vectors of the two given lines.
The cross product is calculated as {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}{i} & {j} & {k} \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & -3 & 2\end{array}\right|{/tex}.
The cross product is {tex}{i}(2 \cdot 2-3 \cdot(-3))-{j}(1 \cdot 2-3 \cdot 0){/tex}{tex}+{k}(1 \cdot(-3)-2 \cdot 0){/tex}.
This simplifies to {tex}{i}(4+9)-{j}(2-0)+{k}(-3-0){/tex}.
The direction vector is {tex}13 {i}-2 {j}-3 {k}{/tex}.
The direction ratios of the required line are {tex}(13,-2,-3){/tex}.

Equation of the Line
The equation of a line passing through a point {tex}\left(x_1, y_1, z_1\right){/tex} with direction ratios {tex}(a, b, c){/tex} is given by {tex}\frac{x-x_1}{a}=\frac{y-y_1}{b}=\frac{z-z_1}{c}{/tex}.
The line passes through the point of intersection {tex}(1,3,5){/tex}.
The direction ratios of the line are {tex}(13,-2,-3){/tex}.
The equation of the line is {tex}\frac{x-1}{13}=\frac{y-3}{-2}=\frac{z-5}{-3}{/tex}.

 5

OR
(ii)

Two vertices of the parallelogram ABCD are given as {tex}{A}(-1,2,1){/tex} and {tex}{B}(1,-2,5){/tex}. If the equation of the line passing through C and D is {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{1}=\frac{{y}+7}{-2}=\frac{{z}-8}{2}{/tex}, then find the distance between sides AB and CD. Hence, find the area of parallelogram ABCD.

Solution:


d.r's of CD are <1, -2, 2>
{tex}\therefore{/tex} d.r's of AB are <1, -2, 2>
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of AB is {tex}\frac{{x}+1}{1}=\frac{{y}-2}{-2}=\frac{{z}-1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Equation of CD is {tex}\frac{{x}-4}{1}=\frac{{y}+7}{-2}=\frac{{z}-8}{2}{/tex}
Let {tex}\overrightarrow{a_1}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+\hat{k},{/tex} {tex} \overrightarrow{a_2}=4 \hat{i}-7 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k} {/tex} & {tex}\vec{b}=\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}=5 \hat{i}-9 \hat{j}+7 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex} \left(\overrightarrow{a_2}-\overrightarrow{a_1}\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 5 & -9 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 2 \end{array}\right|=-4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}-\hat{k} {/tex}
Distance between AB and CD is given by {tex}d=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex}
{tex} d=\frac{\sqrt{16+9+1}}{\sqrt{1+4+4}}=\frac{\sqrt{26}}{3} {/tex}
{tex} C D=\sqrt{2^2+(-4)^2+(4)^2}=6 {/tex}
Area of parallelogram {tex}{ABCD}={b} \times {h}=6 \times \frac{\sqrt{26}}{3}=2 \sqrt{26}{/tex}

 5
33.

Let {tex}{A}={R}-\{5\}{/tex} and {tex}{B}={R}-\{1\}{/tex}. Consider the function {tex}{f}: {A} \rightarrow {B}{/tex}, defined by {tex}{f}(x)=\frac{x-3}{x-5}{/tex}. Show that f is one-one and onto.

Solution:

{tex}\text { Let } f\left(x_{1}\right)=f\left(x_{2}\right), \text { for some } x_{1}, x_{2} \in A{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x_{1}-3}{x_{1}-5}=\frac{x_{2}-3}{x_{2}-5}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(x_{1}-3\right)\left(x_{2}-5\right)=\left(x_{2}-3\right)\left(x_{1}-5\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x_{1}=x_{2},{/tex} So f is one-one Function. 
Let {tex}y=f(x)=\frac{x-3}{x-5} \Rightarrow y(x-5)=x-3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y x-5 y=x-3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{5 y-3}{y-1}{/tex}, We observe that {tex}x{/tex} is defined for all values of y except {tex}y=1{/tex},
So, Range {tex}=R-\{1\}{/tex} and Co-domain is Given {tex}R-\{1\}{/tex} [As, {tex}f: A \rightarrow B{/tex}]
Since, Range {tex}={Co}{/tex}-domain, {tex}f{/tex} is onto Function.
Thus, {tex}f{/tex} is one-one & onto function.

 5
34.(i)

It is given that function {tex}f(x)=x^{4}-62 x^{2}+a x+9{/tex} attains local maximum value at {tex}x=1{/tex}. Find the value of '{tex}a{/tex}', hence obtain all other points where the given function {tex}{f}({x}){/tex} attains local maximum or local minimum values.

Solution:

{tex}f(x)=x^{4}-62 x^{2}+a x+9 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-124 x+a{/tex}
as at {tex}x=1, f{/tex} attains local maximum value, {tex}f^{\prime}(1)=0 \Rightarrow a=120{/tex}
now, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=4 x^{3}-124 x+120={/tex} {tex}4(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x-30\right)=4(x-1)(x-5)(x+6){/tex}
Critical points are {tex}x=-6,1,5{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=12 x^{2}-124{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime \prime}(-6) \gt 0, f^{\prime \prime}(1) \lt 0, f^{\prime \prime}(5) \gt 0{/tex}
so {tex}f{/tex} attains local maximum value at {tex}x=1{/tex} and local minimum value at {tex}x=-6,5{/tex}

 5

OR
(ii)

The perimeter of a rectangular metallic sheet is 300 cm. It is rolled along one of its sides to form a cylinder. Find the dimensions of the rectangular sheet so that volume of cylinder so formed is maximum.

Solution:

To find the dimensions of the rectangular sheet that maximize the volume of the cylinder formed, we start with the given perimeter of the rectangle. The perimeter P of a rectangle is given by the formula:
{tex} P=2(l+w) {/tex}
where {tex}l{/tex} is the length and {tex}w{/tex} is the width. Given that the perimeter is 300 cm, we have:
{tex} 2(l+w)=300 {/tex}
which simplifies to:
{tex} l+w=150 {/tex}
When the rectangle is rolled along one of its sides, we can assume it is rolled along the width w, making the circumference of the cylinder equal to w. The formula for the circumference C of a cylinder is:
{tex} C=2 \pi r {/tex}
where {tex}r{/tex} is the radius. Thus, we have:
{tex} w=2 \pi r {/tex}
The height h of the cylinder will be equal to the length I of the rectangle. The volume V of the cylinder is given by:
{tex} V=\pi r^2 h=\pi r^2 l {/tex}
Substituting I from the perimeter equation, we have:
l = 150 - w
and substituting {tex}w{/tex} in terms of {tex}r{/tex} gives us:
{tex} l=150-2 \pi r {/tex}
Therefore, the volume can be expressed as:
{tex} V=\pi r^2(150-2 \pi r)=150 \pi r^2-2 \pi^2 r^3 {/tex}
To maximize the volume, we take the derivative of V with respect to r and set it to zero:
{tex} \frac{d V}{d r}=300 \pi r-6 \pi^2 r^2=0 {/tex}
Factoring out common terms gives us:
{tex} 3 \pi r(100-2 \pi r)=0 {/tex}
leading to two solutions: {tex}{r}=0{/tex} or {tex}r=\frac{100}{2 \pi}=\frac{50}{\pi}{/tex}. We discard {tex}{r}=0{/tex} as it does not make sense in this context.|
Now, substituting r back to find w and I:

  1. {tex}w=2 \pi r=2 \pi \cdot \frac{50}{\pi}=100{/tex} cm.
  2. {tex}l=150-w=150-100=50{/tex} cm.

Thus, the dimensions of the rectangular sheet are: Length {tex}=50 {~cm}{/tex} and Width {tex}=100 {~cm}{/tex}.

 5
35.

Using integration, find the area of the region enclosed between the curve {tex}y=\sqrt{4-x^{2}}{/tex} and the lines {tex}x=-1, x=1{/tex} and the {tex}x{/tex}-axis.

Solution:


The limits of integration are changed.
When {tex}x=-1, \sin \theta=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}, so {tex}\theta=-\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}.
The integral becomes {tex}A=\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} \sqrt{4-(2 \sin \theta)^2}(2 \cos \theta) d \theta{/tex}.
The expression under the square root is simplified:
{tex} \sqrt{4-4 \sin ^2 \theta}=\sqrt{4\left(1-\sin ^2 \theta\right)}=\sqrt{4 \cos ^2 \theta}=2 \cos \theta {/tex}
The integral is now {tex}A=\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}}(2 \cos \theta)(2 \cos \theta) d \theta=\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} 4 \cos ^2 \theta d \theta{/tex}.
The identity {tex}\cos ^2 \theta=\frac{1+\cos (2 \theta)}{2}{/tex} is used. The integral becomes
{tex} A=\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}} 4\left(\frac{1+\cos (2 \theta)}{2}\right) d \theta=\int\limits_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}}(2+2 \cos (2 \theta)) d \theta {/tex}
The integral is evaluated: {tex}A=[2 \theta+\sin (2 \theta)]_{-\frac{\pi}{6}}^{\frac{\pi}{6}}{/tex}.
The limits are applied: {tex}A=\left(2\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\sin \left(2\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right)\right)-\left(2\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+\sin \left(2\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\right)\right){/tex}

 5

Section - E

This section comprises 3 case study based questions of 4 marks each.

Case Study - 1
36.

A scholarship is a sum of money provided to a student to help him or her pay for education. Some students are granted scholarships based on their academic achievements, while others are rewarded based on their financial needs.

Every year a school offers scholarships to girl children and meritorious achievers based on certain criteria. In the session 2022-23, the school offered monthly scholarship of ₹ 3,000 each to some girl students and ₹ 4,000 each to meritorious achievers in academics as well as sports.
In all, 50 students were given the scholarships and monthly expenditure incurred by the school on scholarships was {tex}₹ 1,80,000{/tex}.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Express the given information algebraically using matrices. (1)
  2. Check whether the system of matrix equations so obtained is consistent or not. (1)
    1. Find the number of scholarships of each kind given by the school, using matrices. (2)

      OR

    2. Had the amount of scholarship given to each girl child and meritorious student been interchanged, what would be the monthly expenditure incurred by the school? (2)
Solution:
  1. Let No. of girl child scholarships {tex}={x}{/tex}
    No. of meritorious achievers {tex}=y{/tex}
    {tex} x+y=50 {/tex}
    {tex} 3000 x+4000 y=180000 \text { or } 3 x+4 y=180 {/tex}
    {tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]} {/tex}
  2. {tex} \left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right|=1 \neq 0 {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} system is consistent.
    1. {tex} \text { Let } {A}=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right], {X}=\left[\begin{array}{l} {x} \\ {y} \end{array}\right], {B}=\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
      {tex} {AX}={B} \Rightarrow {X}={A}^{-1} {~B} {/tex}
      {tex} {X}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 4 & -1 \\ -3 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} 50 \\ 180 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 20 \\ 30 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
      {tex} \Rightarrow {x}=20, {y}=30 {/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex} \text { Required expenditure } {/tex} {tex}=₹[30(3000)+20(4000)] {/tex}
      {tex} =₹ 1,70,000 {/tex}
 4

Case Study - 2
37.

Self-study helps students to build confidence in learning. It boosts the self-esteem of the learners. Recent surveys suggested that close to {tex}50 \%{/tex} learners were self-taught using internet resources and upskilled themselves.

A student may spend 1 hour to 6 hours in a day in upskilling self. The probability distribution of the number of hours spent by a student is given below:
{tex}{P}({X}={x})= \begin{cases}{kx}^{2}, & \text { for } {x}=1,2,3 \\ 2 {kx}, & \text { for } {x}=4,5,6 \\ 0, & \text { otherwise }\end{cases}{/tex}
where x denotes the number of hours.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Express the probability distribution given above in the form of a probability distribution table. (1)
  2. Find the value of k. (1)
    1. Find the mean number of hours spent by the student. (2)

      OR

    2. Find {tex}{P}(1 \lt {X} \lt 6){/tex}. (2)
Solution:
  1. X 1 2 3 4 5 6
    {tex}{P}({X}){/tex} k 4k 9k 8k 10k 12k
  2. {tex} {k}+4 {k}+9 {k}+8 {k}+10 {k}+12 {k}=1 {/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow {k}=\frac{1}{44} {/tex}
    1. {tex} \text { Mean } =\sum x_i p_i{/tex} ={tex}{k}+8 {k}+27 {k}+32 {k}+50 {k}+72 {k} {/tex}
      {tex} =190 {k} {/tex} 
      {tex} =\frac{190}{44} \text { or } \frac{95}{22} {/tex}

      OR

    2. {tex} P(1<X<6) =4 k+9 k+8 k+10 k {/tex}
      {tex} =31 k {/tex}
      {tex} =\frac{31}{44} {/tex}
 4

Case Study - 3
38.

A bacteria sample of certain number of bacteria is observed to grow exponentially in a given amount of time. Using exponential growth model, the rate of growth of this sample of bacteria is calculated.

The differential equation representing the growth of bacteria is given as: {tex}\frac{d P}{d t}=k P{/tex}, where {tex}P{/tex} is the population of bacteria at any time '{tex}t{/tex}'.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Obtain the general solution of the given differential equation and express it as an exponential function of 't'. (2)
  2. If population of bacteria is 1000 at {tex}{t}=0{/tex}, and 2000 at {tex}{t}=1{/tex}, find the value of k. (2)
Solution:
  1. {tex}\frac{d P}{d t}=k P \Rightarrow \int \frac{d P}{P}=\int k d t{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \log P=k t+C \text { or } P=e^{k t+C}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\log P=k t+C{/tex}
    {tex}\text { when } t=0, P= 1000{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow C=\log 1000{/tex}
    {tex}\text { when } t=1, P=2000{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log 2000=k+\log 1000{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow k=\log 2{/tex}
 4
